topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door felt impossibly heavy beneath my hesitant touch. My palms were slick against the cool brass handle, and I could hear the frantic drumming of my own pulse echoing in the quiet hallway. This was it. Dr. Silas Thorne’s office. Silas. The boy who used to dismantle my lunch money with a smirk, now sat behind a desk radiating an almost unnerving calm. I smoothed down my sweater for the tenth time, acutely aware of the slight tremor in my hands. When the door finally swung open, he looked up. His eyes, once sharp with malice, were now deep pools of patient understanding. "Come in," he said, his voice a low, even baritone that seemed to absorb all the surrounding noise. I shuffled across the threshold, my sneakers squeaking faintly on the polished floor, trying desperately to appear composed while my stomach performed a violent, unpredictable flip.

[story 2]

I stood awkwardly by the reception desk, fiddling with the frayed edge of my backpack strap. Every glance Silas Thorne directed my way felt like an interrogation, a silent assessment of my worthiness to occupy his space. I kept my gaze fixed on a water stain on the wall, focusing intensely on the pattern of the discoloration, anything but meeting his gaze. When he finally gestured toward the consultation room, a strange tightening gripped my chest. It was a physical constriction, making it hard to draw a full breath. Walking down the short corridor felt like wading through thick syrup; each step was measured, deliberate, as if I were navigating a minefield. Inside the room, the silence stretched taut between us, amplifying the frantic rush of my thoughts—a chaotic symphony of old resentments and bewildering new curiosity.

[story 3]

"So, you're here," Silas murmured, leaning back in his leather chair, the movement fluid and unhurried. The sound of his voice, so different from the harsh laughter I remembered, settled over me like a warm, heavy blanket, yet it left me feeling exposed. I perched on the edge of the visitor’s chair, my posture stiff, my shoulders hiked up toward my ears. I watched his hands—long, elegant, resting loosely on the desk—and found myself analyzing every minute detail, searching for a flaw, a sign that this transformation was a trick. My throat felt suddenly dry, and I swallowed hard, the movement catching in my chest. "I... I just wanted to see," I managed, the words coming out thin and reedy, barely audible above the hum of the air conditioning.
